title: Chapter 6: Digital Equipment Corporation — The Mainframe's Last Stand (1987–1988)
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: MASTER-EBOOK-ASSEMBLED-v4.md (Chapter 6: Digital Equipment Corporation — The Mainframe's Last Stand (1987-1988))
abstract: Kastner recounts his 1987–1988 tenure at Digital Equipment Corporation as a Marketing Executive, where he orchestrated the DECtp (DEC Transaction Processing) launch and a covert benchmark of IBM's 3090 mainframe that exposed the 'SNA Tax' — massive hidden overhead in IBM's communications stack. He also describes his pivotal role in founding the Transaction Processing Performance Council (TPC), which established independent, audited, industry-standard OLTP benchmarks. The chapter closes with Kastner's observation that DEC, having beaten the mainframe on price-performance, was itself soon to be surpassed by x86 servers using the very same TPC benchmarks.
importance_rationale: The chapter documents firsthand the founding of the TPC — a benchmark body whose standards shaped enterprise server procurement for decades — and the covert competitive intelligence operation that exposed the SNA Tax, materially affecting IBM's market position.
relevance_rationale: Central to the history of OLTP benchmarking, enterprise minicomputer marketing, and the transition from mainframe to minicomputer to x86 server architectures in the late 1980s and early 1990s.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 1987 Yankee Group report predicted mainframe displacement by multiprocessor Intel 386 systems in the early 1990s, which proved accurate; he also foresaw that x86 would displace the VAX architecture DEC was promoting — a prediction confirmed within four years.
